item.title: Staff Post- Open
item.content:encoded:
Noting that at the request of the author, edits have been made to the article.

Edit: Since there has been some questions/concerns on the subject, also noting that I did this per precedent on the subject and at the instruction and auspices of the Disciplinary Team. This was not done unilaterally, was not done in a Rewrite Team capacity, and I took no pleasure in having to make these edits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I admit that when I first upvoted this I knew virtually nothing about Tupac. Now that I’m more familiar with the man and his work, the only thing I feel toward this article is sheer embarrassment.

item.title: Here's my opinion
item.content:encoded:
Despite all the…

idk, "things" that the writer did, I still kinda like this SCP-Entry.

But the thing that really bothers me about it is the 24/7 use of ██ which gets really annoying. The other thing that SORTA bothers me is 2pac being part of the law even tho he wasn't in real life, but I think is was just trying to say that this ghost of 2pac didin't want people to suffer what he suffered when he was killed. But this is coming from his "Me against the world" album which sorta defeats the purpose of him helping people.

I get why people didn't like the use of the N word being used in the SCP, but honestly I don't really mind it sense its coming from a really legendary rapper. If it Eminem instead of 2pac, I would be extremely bothered by it though.

Also this reminds me of Dave Chappelles sketch on 2pac lol

I WOULD say to rewrite this but, what is there to rewrite?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yep. I'm revisiting it to primarily criticize the n-word usage in particular. It's such a poor attempt at being edgy. If only anything else about the author or his work could have clued us into that.

This article has not stood the test of time. Hope the author has worse times ahead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In retrospect, Kalinin was right, and this was never all that good.

Also, and this is purely my opinion, having an entitled white man repeatedly use the n-word, uncensored, in his article is a little uncomfortable in retrospect. It would be one thing if it were merely used for hip-hop verisimilitude, or character development but given what we now know about the author’s moral character it just looks like assholish flaunting of taboo.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The image was sourced to here, which protects all images hosted under All Rights Reserved. As such, the image is not compliant with CC 3.0 and is pending staff evaluation/removal.

Edit: Image has since been removed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I honestly think there is some merit to this entity, though some of the writing leaves much to be desired it's not un-salvageable. I think re-writing this one is possible without it loosing it's charm.
My least favorite of the "Songs": SCP-2137-1-564 however needs to either be filled in or rewritten in it's entirety. Simply put It looks like a freaking bar-code.

The sheer number of ██ is a tad excessive overall in the article.

But to put my two cents in: the likely reason the "Tupac Spirit" is the market for unsolved crimes is because Tupac's own death is more or less an unsolved crime itself. Meaning that it either wants vengeance or to protect others from the same fate.

I also note the fact the presence of "Guest stars" means that SCP-2137-2 is not just Tupac's voice but can be anyone's voice. It just has a preference for acting as Tupac.
Also he does not actually write raps for the cops specifically, The means of bringing the killer to light are not that important, It simply wants justice served so to speak.

These are important ideas that are not clearly addressed in the article proper. Also on the premise that is an Anomalous Object behavioral discrepancies between it and the actual Tupac could be considered be part of it's properties.
Overall i'd say that there is plenty that could be salvaged here, there are interesting ideas but are either muddied or omit important details.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: alas i have nought but a downvote to offer here

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dude linked the article on his official Twitter back when he first wrote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He's mentioned writing for the site numerous times, as well as repeat his adoration for SCP and its universe before, such as on Twitter, and various podcasts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're right that it could be a troll, but I'm perplexed why, of all the famous people they could choose, they would pick a sort of vaguely known writer like Max Landis (especially five years ago when he was even less famous).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there good reason to believe this member was actually Max Landis?

Too much time among the trolls has made me assume everyone is lying about their identity until conclusively proven otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Blinded by a combination of hero worship with a competently-written article had a lot of people fooled. Unfortunately, I admit I was one of them, but even without hindsight or taking into account Max Landis's atrocities, it seems evident that this SCP has aged about as well as bread left out in hot, humid weather to rot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is easily one of my favorite comments sections to read through with the benefit of hindsight. The absolute gargling that was taking place borders on obscenity.

Just a reminder that these are some of the hard hitting lyrics in this article:

Help me catch my killers
And I'll let you have yours
Trust me, you nerds do not want a war

Yeah. Tupac. This whole thing reads like one of the worse episodes of Epic Rap Battles. Look at some actual Pac lyrics:

I see no changes all I see is racist faces
Misplaced hate makes disgrace to races
We under I wonder what it takes to make this
One better place, let's erase the wasted

Nah dude. No way.

The shit you mention about him writing raps for the cops is hysterical too, considering this is supposed to be the same dude who wrote:

Cops give a damn about a negro
Pull the trigger kill a nigga he's a hero
Give the crack to the kids who the hell cares
One less hungry mouth on the welfare

Why did we ever think this article was ok?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reading through the comments on this one and seeing all the people starstruck over a quasi-famous person who even at the time was clearly here for a quick shot of narcissistic supply is embarrassing. Even without what we now know about him.

The thing that's always stuck in my craw is that for all the fawning, this isn't even a good article. No real attempt to contextualize why Tupac Shakur, who had numerous contacts with the American legal system which included being incarcerated and being beaten for jaywalking by cops, would suddenly take an interest in directly assisting law enforcement from beyond the grave. Mostly because this was the product of a shallow understanding of the subject material and a hasty write-up. And good lord, the rap lyrics in this. It feels like someone ran doggerel through Gizoogle.

No understanding of the person that's being written about. No understanding of hip-hop, either in spirit (can you imagine a rap artist releasing an album called "Assist the Police"?) or artistic structure. Barely an understanding, even in the earlier era in which this written, of what constitutes a good SCP article beyond the immediately identifiable tropes (guys in orange jump suits, scary keter monsters locked up somewhere, spooky XK). I don't know why I never documented my thoughts on this article back when it was first posted, but that was clearly a mistake, because I sense a forthcoming discussion on separating the artist from the product, when the product isn't really worth a whole lot on its own in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not a "drama", it's the author, Max Landis, being a known abuser of women and general creep.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I'd like it better as being released under Kanye's name, honestly. For one thing, that helps with the plausible deniability - Kanye's got some issues, and this could be more easily swept under the rug if any remaining copies were explained away as another of Kanye's experimental moments.

I'm still bugged by the MTF departing from standard nomenclature, that is a Greek letter and a two-digit number - "MTF Delta-16" or some such, rather than "MTF-339". Perhaps the number is of significance in Tupac's work, but if it is I for one certainly wouldn't know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no idea about the drama discussed here in the comments, but honestly the scp is a work of art. Now i'm not a rap or hip-hop fan but the amount of effort and thoughtfulness put in here is huge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I can't stand how the author has been accused of multiple instances of sexual abuse by multiple people.

Also, that's generally how Max's writing is in general. Starts out fine, ends up an annoying mess. And I like more of his work than not. Just the reality of his work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept is good, the write is ok. But I simply couldn't stand how the article evolves. Neither do I think the Foundation would be so petty as to evade proper procedures because "the skip shouldn't give orders", nor its supreme awesomeness convinced me. It started good and became simply annoying.

item.title: Typo?
item.content:encoded:
However, the track is easily recorded through conventional means, recorded versions are not subject to change or replacement, and the original can be replayed from the CD endlessly before the killer is found, with no little variation. There are currently nearly one thousand instances of SCP-2137-2 on record.

Should be SCP-2137-1, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: fuck the author but 2pac did nothing wrong

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Regardless though, I don't see how that's relevant, there's certain to be works on the wiki by people who have done awful things, whether we know it or not. We are supposed to consider the work, not the author.

Well put. I couldn't agree more.
Also was unaware of that interview so thanks for letting me know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
His icky "drunk" interview definitely happened, regardless of what you think about the accusations.

Regardless though, I don't see how that's relevant, there's certain to be works on the wiki by people who have done awful things, whether we know it or not. We are supposed to consider the work, not the author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "sins of the author"? In everything I've read about those sexual assault allegations they were just that, allegations. I haven't read or seen anything proving or disproving them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Despite the sins of the author, this SCP was an excellent addition to our site and I can still appreciate it artistically (of course I completely understand if others disagree, given what happened). That said, I would be highly reluctant to use this in a tale simply because of the stigma now attached to it, so I think it's fine to let it float on its own and let people make of it what they will.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That would be black white black white black white black white black white grey

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a great SCP. Tone and pacing are superb here. I honestly want more famous people to write on the wiki. Imagine if that became a thing?

This comment aged poorly. A reread of this SCP doesn't impress me as much.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This got my upvote so hard and I didn't want to like it at first but it brings the anomalous game hard.

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
It had to be done at some point, and now it has happened!

https://youtu.be/s6utCWTxi9c

On the Skip itself, I greatly appreciate the Foundation being cajoled into doing good against their will. Every time AWCY tries to talk down to the Foundation for containing things but not doing enough to save people, I think of this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"This is a confession, admissable in court!
I killed Darnell Simmons for sport!"
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't believe you didn't say "Weezy F Baby and the F is for Foundation".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is my new favorite, and not just because I'm a huge 2Pac fan (though that certainly helps). I feel like if 2Pac's ghost was going to be doing anything, it would be giving people hints to unsolved crimes. After all, his murder's still unsolved, so it makes sense.

Also, if someone can do a really good 2Pac rap impression, and has good sound editing, they should seriously try and record the lyrics in this one. I can totally hear 2Pac's voice when I read them, and would love to hear a 2Pac "sound-alike" recording.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sooooooooo… the ghost of Tupac is trapped in CD and uses his cosmic ghost powers to solve crimes…. just another day at the office for The SCP Foundation!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I… no words.

Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely adore this, oh my god. I am not sure if 'laughing out loud at the audaciousness of some of these things' was the reaction you were going for, but the escalation really was just marvelous. I like this album that may or may not be the actual ghost of Tupac quite a lot. Well done - upvoted with a vengeance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved that graygraygrayblackwhite (forgot name) tale reference.
Up votes for cool concept! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As of this moment, this is by far my favorite article on this site.

Also, MTF "The Suge Knights" is art. Pure art.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now what will happen if the O5s decide to snap the CD into halves?

Anyway, good one. I have no clue how 2pac's lyrics will be like, but the one u came out with is plain awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Think I might have just missed this one when I trawled through all the series 2s a while back (Iread SCP in fits and starts) - came to it through 978's experiment logs (one of my favorites, because of all the cross-links).

I really like the idea that the Foundation have actually encountered something that is basically forcefully (sort-of) benevolent and making them do a bit of active good, whether they like it or not…!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love, love, love that the ghost of Tupac on a cd is blackmailing the Foundation into helping solve crimes.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hahaha, this is so goofy, but still really good.
I don't listen to hip hop, but I like this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know I'm responding to a 2 year old post, but that may be the best description of this site there is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know how I didn't find this before but +1 to infinity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ha!
I was waiting for it to get ridiculous and silly, but it never did and I ended up imagining 2pac actually rapping those lyrics (in fact, they are very plausible; they quite show some similarity with 2pac's works), feeling weirdly defeated. You won, you monster.

item.title: Serious?
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure whether to laugh or take this seriously. It's very well written, despite the totally ridiculous nature of it's anomalous properties. By the way, "XK Scenario" sounds like a great name for either a Hip-Hop or heavy metal album.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it before it started showing self-awareness, so that was like icing on the cake. It could've been a nice Euclid or Safe SCP, but you managed a solid Keter. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am extremely impressed that you managed to make a CD a Keter-class SCP. (slow clap)

Also: those lyrics are fucking amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lol Lil Wayne in an SCP. Fuckin' great. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Call me old-fashioned but I would really like to hear what an Andrews Sisters/Tupac crossover would sound like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, thought I commented on this one when I first upvoted it, guess I didn't. AH well, I still think it's amazing. Normally I'm a bit leery of write-ups when the Skips turn out to be helpful, but in this case it still works, partly because - while we know what the Skip claims are its goals - we still can't be certain whether those actually are the goals, and partly because just the act of containing it forces the Foundation into a somewhat reluctant crime-fighter position. So in the end, we wind up with an apparently benevolent Skip, but not one that's automatically willing to play ball with the Foundation. I like this, I really do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have just read this and… NOW ONE OF MY FAVORITE SCPs

About time an scp showed the foundation that they were made to help so they should help. Also it's cool to see it put the O5 council in its place

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot about this that I love. Sure I could talk about how it was well written or the fact that it took a crazy premise and executed it perfectly, but what really made this one stand out for me were two things.

1. The ending left me feeling satisfied. So often, I feel like great SCP's end before they get to "the good stuff." Here you ended with just the right amount of mystery and closure.

2. I really like the ethical issue you introduced between the Foundation not wanting to serve the SCP's and the obvious benefits of getting serial killers and murderers off the streets.

This is easily one of my top ten favorite SCP's in the new series. Incredible job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP reminds me a ton of SCP-871; a completely stupid premise on paper ("The ghost of 2pac is a vigilante murder investigator and communicates through one of his rap albums!") that was somehow made awesome by clever and strong writing. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed with the nitpick. Even with SCP's competence and resources, a complete cover up would be extremely difficult if not impossible here. I'd have preferred something along the lines of "We kept rumors of the video off the web and mostly contained in the Los Angeles area. With counter-rumors and select memory wipes of particularly outspoken witnesses, we managed to spin the existence of the video into an urban legend." Or maybe SCP promptly made a similar video without incriminating lines, and encouraged rumors about that "hoax" to cover the details of the real video, or something. I'd like to think the SCP has better responses than SHIT SHIT SHIT MINDWIPE EVERYONE in these scenarios.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is probably my favorite SCP that's been posted during my absence. Enthusiastically +1ed!

One nitpick: the way in which 2137's containment breach is covered up stretches credibility. Given how many people viewed the video, and the sheer amount of resources required to cover it up, it would be very difficult to keep it under wraps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man this is one of my new favorites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I logged in from work just to upvote. Sir, you are a genius.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept, I like the execution, but what surprises me the most about this article is one of the little things.

For my whole freshman year of high school, every schoolday, I was in my brother's car, listening to modern rap. As a result, I am very familiar with Lil Wayne's flow. And you nailed it. Pac's lyrics, I could hear in Pac's voice and all, that was good, but hoool-eeee crap. When I was reading Lil Wayne's part, I didn't just hear his voice. I heard every minute detail of how he'd do a Foundation rap, right down to the scratchy giggle he does before verses.
You, sir, know your Weezy.

One thing though

This has included many of Tupac's contemporaries, such as Snoop Doggy Dogg, Nas, No Doubt, Everclear, Weezer and Busta Rhymes

You forgot about Dre!

Also, now that I think about it, this whole thing sounds like it was inspired by a bit from a Chris Rock stand-up show from back in 2005. And I hope that's the case, because if so, it just makes everything about this SCP even more hilarious while still being fantastic and creepy and weird and just quintessential of all the major elements that make a great article.

"It was a nigga named Kevin with a Mac Eleven..."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is absolutely amazing! I just can't even believe you somehow managed to make this thing work! I love the lyrics, they really wrap it all up for me.

Also, it is just too cool to see Max Landis on this site… It makes me wanna cry.

Amazing job, man.
